projects.title: Mama Nourish
projects.summary: From 40 orders a month to 1300+
body: Mama Nourish was doing around forty orders a month — enough to prove people wanted the product, not enough to be a business.
The constraint was trust. Nutrition aimed at mothers is a category where claims are cheap and scepticism is the default setting, so performance spend against cold audiences burns out fast.

Working the creative and the targeting together took it past 1300 orders a month. The lever was not budget; it was finding which specific message survived contact with a genuinely sceptical audience, then putting everything behind it.

projects.title: eBullion
projects.summary: Making precious metals feel like an asset class, not a shop
body: Precious metals investment sits in an awkward place: too serious for lifestyle content, too unfamiliar for finance content aimed at equity investors.
We built the social and SEO presence around education — what platinum and palladium are for, how allocation actually works, why someone would hold bullion at all.
That answers the question a first-time buyer has before they will consider the platform, and it compounds: search demand for those questions exists whether or not anyone is running ads that week.

projects.title: Hip Hop Skincare
projects.summary: Reviving a brand known for exactly one product
body: Hip Hop is known for its blackhead remover strips — genuinely iconic, and the whole problem, because everything else the brand made lived in that product’s shadow.

Rather than a general awareness push we built around a specific sale event. A deadline gives people a reason to act now instead of filing you under maybe later, and gives creators something concrete to talk about.

Creator partnerships carried discovery while paid stayed pointed at value propositions. In a category this crowded with Mamaearth and Sugar setting the pace, the brand story is not what closes the sale.

projects.title: Sutlej Textiles and Industries Limited
projects.summary: B2B digital for one of India’s largest textile manufacturers
body: Sutlej is one of India’s most established textile manufacturers, and its buyers are procurement teams rather than consumers — a long cycle, a small audience, and a category with almost no digital convention to borrow from.

B2B social usually fails by imitating B2C energy. We went the other way: content that respected how technical the audience is, built around capability, capacity and certification rather than lifestyle.
The point was never volume. It was being visible and legible to a few hundred people who matter, at the moment they are shortlisting.

projects.title: Nesterra Home Decor
projects.summary: A digital story for a home decor brand with none
body: Home decor is bought visually and emotionally, but most decor brands online look like a catalogue with better photography.
Nesterra needed a digital story rather than a product feed — a reason to follow the brand between purchases, given nobody buys home textiles monthly.
We built content around occasions and seasons instead of SKUs. The product appears, but it is not the subject; the room is.

projects.title: The Big Barbeque
projects.summary: Buffet restaurants across Chennai and Bangalore
body: A buffet chain across Chennai and Bangalore competes on a very short decision: where are we eating tonight.

That makes social a proximity and appetite channel rather than a brand channel. The work was disciplined food photography, posted with the timing of the decision rather than the timing of a content calendar.
Two cities also means two audiences with different food cultures, so the same asset rarely worked in both. Most of the effort went into knowing when to split.

projects.title: Chenab Gourmet
projects.summary: Imported gourmet, sold direct for the first time
body: Chenab supplied the kitchens behind some of India’s best restaurants until lockdown switched that business off overnight. Going direct to consumers stopped being a growth idea and became a survival plan.

The asset nobody had noticed was exclusive Indian distribution for Camerons, Maple Joe, Hugo Reitzel, Tate & Lyle, iSi and Coravin — a catalogue of things you genuinely could not buy on a marketplace.
The creative stopped talking about products and started talking about access. That reframing changed who bought: serious home cooks who had been looking for exactly this and had given up finding it.

projects.title: Big Bazaar Food
projects.summary: Food retail at national scale
body: Food retail at national scale has a specific problem: the offer changes weekly, the audience is everyone, and the creative has to work in eleven contexts at once.
We built a system rather than campaigns — templates with enough structural discipline to stay recognisably one brand while carrying a completely different promotion every few days.
The measure of success here is not a single hero post. It is whether a year of daily output still looks like it came from one place.

projects.title: Beardo
projects.summary: Three products on Amazon to a Marico acquisition
body: When Beardo came to us they were a startup with three products newly listed on Amazon. The category barely existed in India — grooming for bearded men was not a market with share to take, it was a behaviour that needed normalising first.

We built the brand communications around strong imagery and deliberately risqué content aimed at real men rather than an idealised version of them. Within a year the visual language and tone of voice were set, and they are still in use today.

We also built and ran the website, handled order management and backend operations, ran the entire social communication, managed media spend, and produced collateral down to hoardings and mailers. Three years of that groundwork is what brought national recognition — and, we suspect, Marico’s attention.

projects.title: Society Tea
projects.summary: A heritage tea brand talking to a younger drinker
body: Society Tea is a genuinely heritage brand, which is an asset with an expiry date if the next generation of drinkers reads it as their parents’ tea.

The work was tonal more than structural. Heritage had to stop being the message and start being the backdrop — present in the craft and the sourcing, absent from the register, which needed to sound like now.
Daily social at volume, built around the ritual rather than the product. Tea is rarely bought on specification; it is bought on the fifteen minutes it belongs to.

projects.title: Taurus Mutual Funds
projects.summary: Mutual funds explained without condescension
body: Financial services marketing in India tends to pick one of two registers: intimidating, or condescending.
Taurus needed neither. Mutual funds are bought by people who are cautious rather than ignorant, and who resent being spoken to as beginners.

The work was explanatory content that assumed intelligence and no jargon — a harder brief than it sounds, and the reason most fund marketing reads like a compliance document with a stock photo on top.

projects.title: Prolicious
projects.summary: Health food that had to look worth eating
body: Prolicious sells health food into a category where almost every competitor photographs their product like medicine — clinical, white background, ingredient callouts as the hero.
We shot and wrote it the other way: as food first, credentials second. If a snack doesn't look worth eating, the protein count on the label never gets read.

The content calendar ran on cravings and moments (the 4pm slump, the pre-workout snack) rather than nutritional categories, since that's the actual decision a buyer is making when they stop scrolling.

projects.title: Frinza
projects.summary: Flowers and gifting, built for occasions
body: Flowers and gifting is a category with almost no loyalty — most orders are a single occasion, chosen under time pressure, and the brand is rarely the reason someone picked a florist twice.

Frinza's content ran on the occasion calendar rather than the catalogue: anniversaries, apology bouquets, first-day-of-job gifts, each with its own tone rather than one generic 'flowers for every mood' feed.
The job was to be top of mind at the exact moment someone remembers they've left it too late — which meant posting rhythm mattered as much as the creative itself.

projects.title: The Complete Man
projects.summary: US men’s grooming, run from Mumbai
body: Running US-facing men's grooming from a Mumbai studio means the content has to read as native to an audience that will never see the team making it.

We leaned on category research rather than assumption — American grooming content skews towards routine and results, not the humour-first tone that works in Indian social — and built the account's voice around that.
Consistency mattered more than any single viral swing here: a grooming routine is a habit brand, and habit brands are built on being seen often, not seen once.

projects.title: Bare Body Essentials
projects.summary: Concern-led cosmetics without the clinical tone
body: Concern-led skincare (acne, pigmentation, sensitivity) tends to default to a clinical register that reads more like a dermatology pamphlet than a brand.
Bare Body Essentials needed the opposite: real skin concerns taken seriously without the fear-based framing so much of the category leans on.

The content spoke to the concern honestly, then let the product be the practical answer rather than the emotional hook — a smaller, more trustworthy claim than most competitors make, and one worth keeping.

projects.title: Sugarwatchers Digital Marketing
projects.summary: Low-GI food for people managing sugar
body: Low-GI food sits at an odd intersection: it's bought by people managing a health condition, but nobody wants their feed to feel like a diagnosis.
Sugarwatchers' content stayed practical — what to eat, not what to avoid — since a category built entirely on restriction reads as punishment rather than a brand you want to follow.
The audience skews older and more risk-aware than most D2C food buyers, so claims stayed conservative and specific rather than reaching for the usual wellness superlatives.

projects.title: Unicorn Finance
projects.summary: DeFi explained without the jargon
body: DeFi marketing has a language problem — most of it is written by people who already understand the product, for an audience that mostly doesn't.
Unicorn Finance's social had to do translation work first and persuasion second: what a swap actually is, why liquidity matters, before any feature could land.
The tone stayed plain rather than crypto-native, on the theory that anyone still confused by the jargon is also still deciding whether to trust the category at all.

projects.title: Mermaid Swap
projects.summary: Character-led social for a multi-chain swap
body: Multi-chain swap products are functionally similar to each other, so Mermaid Swap's social had to win on personality rather than feature comparison.
We built the account around a consistent character and visual world instead of the usual DeFi mix of charts and jargon, giving the brand something to be recognised by beyond its function.
That character carried the account through the market's usual volatility — content people follow for the voice keeps working even in weeks when the news is bad.

projects.title: Epic Media Labs Socal Media
projects.summary: Social for a media lab that builds media
body: Running social media for a media lab is an unusual brief: the client's own credibility depends on the account looking like it was made by people who understand media, not marketers who've been briefed on it.
We treated Epic Media Labs' feed as a showcase of craft first and a promotional channel second — the content had to be good enough to justify the client's own expertise.
That standard raised the bar on production value across the board, which is the right problem to have when your client's business is producing media for other people.

projects.title: Isvaari
projects.summary: Occasion dressing with a seasonal calendar
body: Occasion dressing is bought against a date, not a mood, so Isvaari's content calendar was built backwards from India's festival and wedding season rather than a generic monthly plan.
Each occasion got its own visual language — Diwali reads differently to a sangeet, which reads differently to a college farewell — instead of one seasonal palette stretched across all of them.
The discipline was in the lead time: dressing content has to land while someone is still deciding what to wear, not after they've already bought it.

projects.title: KVL Denim
projects.summary: Denim manufacturing with a consumer face
body: KVL manufactures denim at scale for other brands, which meant the challenge was building a consumer-facing presence for a business that had never needed one before.
We built the account around process and craft — fabric, fit, construction — rather than pretending to be a fashion label, since credibility here comes from manufacturing expertise, not trend-chasing.
That honesty about what KVL actually is turned out to be the more interesting story than a manufactured lifestyle brand would have been.

projects.title: Cruise Air Conditioners Landing Page
projects.summary: A landing page built for one season
body: A landing page built for one season has a specific constraint: it has to convert during a short, predictable demand spike and can't rely on being discovered gradually the way an evergreen site can.

We built Cruise's page around the two questions a buyer actually has in peak summer — will this cool my specific room, and how fast can I get it installed — and cut everything that didn't answer one of those.
Speed mattered as much as copy: a slow-loading page in the exact week everyone is shopping for an AC is a page that loses the sale to whoever loads first.

projects.title: Healthvit Ecommerce Website
projects.summary: Supplements commerce at catalogue scale
body: Supplements commerce at catalogue scale is a search-and-filter problem before it's a design problem — hundreds of SKUs across dozens of health concerns, and most visitors arrive already knowing roughly what they need.
We built Healthvit's storefront around fast, confident navigation to a specific product rather than a browsing experience, since supplement buyers convert on certainty, not discovery.

Trust signals (batch testing, certifications, plain-language dosage info) sat closer to the buy button than is typical for the category, since hesitation at checkout is the real conversion risk in supplements.

projects.title: Zephyr Perfumes
projects.summary: Fragrance sold on a screen
body: Fragrance is the hardest thing to sell online — the entire product is a sense a screen can't reproduce.
Zephyr's content worked around that by selling the moment a scent is worn in, rather than the scent itself: getting ready, a specific occasion, the last thing you do before leaving the house.
That's a deliberate substitution — you can't show smell, but you can show the feeling of wearing something you like, and let that stand in for it.

projects.title: Branding Projects
projects.summary: Identity systems across categories
body: Identity work across categories — grooming, food, finance, fashion — means there's no single visual formula to reach for; each brand needs a system built from its own category conventions, then deliberately broken where it matters.

The common thread across these projects wasn't a house style, it was a process: understand what the category expects, decide what to keep and what to challenge, then build a mark and system flexible enough to survive years of use.
A logo is the smallest part of an identity system. Most of the work — and most of what actually holds a brand together — is the rules around how it's used.

projects.title: Volkano
projects.summary: Consumer tech with a challenger voice
body: Consumer tech is a category where every brand claims to be the challenger, which makes an actual challenger voice hard to hear over the noise.

Volkano's content leaned into direct comparison and plain specs rather than the genre's usual lifestyle-first approach, betting that a tech buyer researching a purchase wants information before inspiration.
That's a bet on the audience being further down the funnel than most consumer tech marketing assumes — someone who's already narrowing options, not someone being introduced to the category.

projects.title: Nutrizoe
projects.summary: Nutrition for mothers, not patients
body: Nutrition for mothers gets marketed as medicine more often than it needs to be — clinical claims, deficiency-first messaging, a tone that assumes something is wrong.

Nutrizoe's content treated the audience as capable adults making a considered choice, not patients being informed of a problem, which meant leading with what the product does for daily energy and routine rather than what it corrects.
That register shift is subtle but it changes who the brand feels like it's for — a wellness choice, not a diagnosis.

projects.title: Swayamacare
projects.summary: Care products with a plain-spoken tone
body: Care products in this category tend to over-explain, stacking claim after claim until the actual message gets lost in the label copy.

Swayamacare's content stripped that back to plain, direct statements about what the product is for and who it's for, on the theory that clarity reads as more trustworthy than a longer list of benefits.
Plain-spoken doesn't mean unpolished — the discipline was saying less, more precisely, not saying less effort went in.

projects.title: F5 Smart Tech
projects.summary: Smart tech for homes that aren’t smart yet
body: Smart home tech is usually sold to people who already consider themselves early adopters — a narrow audience in a market where most homes aren't smart yet at all.
F5's content worked to make the category legible to a first-time buyer: what a smart device actually changes about a normal day, not a feature list assuming familiarity with the category.

The real shoot behind this campaign leaned on showing the product in an ordinary home rather than a show-home set, since that's the gap between where the market is and where most smart-tech marketing assumes it already is.

projects.title: Healthvit
projects.summary: Supplements, daily social at volume
body: Daily social at volume for a supplements brand with hundreds of SKUs means the real skill is a repeatable system, not a single hero campaign.

We built templates disciplined enough to stay recognisably one brand across dozens of different products and concerns, so the daily posting rhythm never looked like it was scrambling to fill a calendar.
Consistency compounds in a catalogue business like this — a customer who trusts the brand's voice on one product is more likely to trust it on the next fifty.

projects.title: Only Solitaires
projects.summary: Solitaires online, where trust is everything
body: Buying a solitaire online is a trust problem before it's a design problem — high value, hard to verify by photo alone, and a category with a real history of substituted stones and inflated certifications.

Only Solitaires' content leaned hard into transparency: certification, sourcing, and plain explanation of what makes a stone worth its price, rather than the aspirational styling most jewellery marketing defaults to.
For a purchase this considered, the brand that explains the most, most plainly, tends to win the sale — glamour photography alone doesn't close it.

projects.title: The Big Indian
projects.summary: A restaurant brand with a point of view
body: A restaurant brand competing purely on cuisine photography looks like every other restaurant account; The Big Indian needed a point of view that wasn't just 'the food is good.'
We built the content around a specific stance on Indian food — regional, unapologetic, not softened for a general palate — giving the account something to actually stand for beyond appetite appeal.
That point of view is what gets shared and argued about, which does more for a restaurant's visibility than another well-lit thali ever will.

projects.title: Eves 24
projects.summary: Women’s care with a frank register
body: Women's care products are one of the last categories where marketing still defaults to euphemism — blue liquid, vague language, discomfort with the actual subject.
Eves 24's content spoke plainly and directly about what the product does and why, treating the audience as adults rather than working around the topic the way the category conventionally does.
That frankness is a differentiator by default in a category where almost every competitor is still speaking in code.

projects.title: Uspray Toilet Seat Sanitizer
projects.summary: A pandemic product with an awkward category
body: A toilet seat sanitizer is an easy product to be embarrassed about marketing, and a pandemic-era launch meant the category itself had almost no existing convention to follow.

We built Uspray's content around the practical anxiety the product actually solves — shared and public bathrooms — rather than reaching for humour to defuse an awkward category, since the moment called for reassurance, not a joke.
Being straightforward about a slightly awkward product read as more credible than trying too hard to make it cool.

projects.title: Jewelry Fairs By UBM
projects.summary: Trade fairs that needed a trade audience
body: Trade fairs sell to a professional audience — buyers, wholesalers, exhibitors — not consumers, which means the usual jewellery-marketing playbook of glamour and aspiration is close to irrelevant here.

The content for UBM's jewellery fairs stayed focused on what a trade attendee actually needs to decide whether to show up: who's exhibiting, what's new in the category, the commercial case for attending.
A B2B audience this specific rewards being useful over being beautiful — the content earned attention by being informative first.

projects.title: DaadhiMooch
projects.summary: Grooming with a regional accent
body: Grooming is a crowded category nationally, and DaadhiMooch's opportunity was a regional accent most national grooming brands don't bother with.
The content leaned into that specificity — language, references, a tone that read as made for a particular audience rather than a diluted version of a pan-India campaign.
Being unmistakably for someone, rather than acceptable to everyone, is what let a smaller brand compete against grooming players with far bigger budgets.

projects.title: Embark Perfumes
projects.summary: Fragrance for people who travel
body: Embark's positioning around travel gave fragrance marketing something most competitors in the category don't have: a specific use-occasion rather than a general mood.

The content built around that — the flight, the new city, the version of yourself that a new place lets you be — using travel as the frame the scent lived inside rather than an abstract lifestyle backdrop.
Anchoring an intangible product (scent) to a concrete moment (travel) gave the audience something to actually picture, which most fragrance marketing struggles to do.

projects.title: Naturevibe India
projects.summary: The home market for a US-led brand
body: Naturevibe built its identity in the US market first; bringing that same brand home to India meant adjusting for a domestic audience already familiar with the category rather than one being introduced to it.

The Indian market content leaned more on ingredient-specific credibility and pricing clarity than the US arm's creator-led approach, since Indian wellness buyers are typically more price- and provenance-conscious.

The real shoot behind this campaign gave the India launch its own visual identity rather than repurposing the US creative wholesale — a decision that mattered more than it might sound, since a market this different needed to feel locally made.

projects.title: Culture Truck
projects.summary: Culture commerce for a young audience
body: Culture commerce aimed at a young audience lives or dies on cultural fluency — a brand that's slightly behind the reference points it's using reads as trying too hard, instantly.
Culture Truck's content moved at the pace the audience actually consumes culture, prioritising speed and relevance over the longer production cycles more traditional retail marketing runs on.
That trade-off — less polish, more timing — is the right one for an audience that values being early to something over being shown something perfectly finished.

projects.title: Cruise Air Conditioners
projects.summary: Cooling, sold on comfort not specs
body: Air conditioning is bought on specifications by the time someone's actually comparing units, but the earlier decision — considering an upgrade at all — is emotional: comfort, a hot night, a room that's never been the temperature you want.
Cruise's ongoing social content lived in that earlier moment, selling the feeling of a room finally being comfortable before the audience got to the spec-comparison stage a landing page could handle.
Splitting those two jobs — feeling first, specification later — meant neither piece of content had to do the other's work.

projects.title: Chikaku
projects.summary: Kids’ brand that had to charm parents
body: Kids' brands have two audiences in every piece of content: the child who wants the product and the parent who has to approve the purchase, and those two people respond to almost nothing the same way.

Chikaku's content was built to satisfy both simultaneously — playful and colourful enough to catch a child's attention, but backed by the safety and material information a parent actually checks before buying.
Charm alone sells nothing in this category if the parent doesn't trust what's behind it, so the reassurance had to sit as close to the delight as the product allowed.

projects.title: That’s Personal
projects.summary: Social & Content for That’s Personal
body: That's Personal operates in a category built on discretion, where a brand's tone matters as much as anything it actually says — too clinical reads as cold, too casual reads as careless.
The content struck a plain, respectful register: direct enough to be useful, careful enough not to embarrass anyone consuming it in a public feed.

Getting that balance right is most of the job in a personal-care category like this one — the product claims matter less than whether the audience feels comfortable being seen following the account at all.

projects.title: Social Media Designs
projects.summary: Design systems for social, across clients
body: Design systems built for social have to survive being used by people other than the designer who made them — templates need enough structure to stay on-brand and enough flexibility to still work three months and fifty posts later.

Across these client engagements the brief was consistent even when the brands weren't: build a visual system disciplined enough to hold together at daily-posting volume without needing a designer to sign off on every single post.
The measure of a good social design system isn't how good the first ten posts look — it's whether post four hundred still looks like it belongs to the same brand.

projects.title: Total Snacc
projects.summary: A high-protein, roasted-not-fried bhujia making the case against the regular stuff
body: Roasted-not-fried bhujia is making an argument against an entire category's manufacturing convention, which means the content has to do more explaining than a typical snack launch.
We built the case plainly — same craving, same crunch, a genuinely different process — rather than leaning on generic 'healthy snacking' language that every competitor already uses.
The real shoot behind this campaign leaned into texture and process shots specifically, since 'roasted not fried' is a claim people trust more when they can see it, not just read it.

projects.title: The Gaming Truck
projects.summary: A mobile gaming rig that turns up for kids' birthdays and office team days alike
body: A mobile gaming rig is an unusual product to market — the thing being sold is an event experience, not an object, and it has to work for a kids' birthday party and a corporate team day with the same rig and a completely different pitch.

The content split cleanly along that line: play and excitement for the birthday-party audience, team-building and novelty for the corporate booking audience, using the same real shoot footage cut two different ways.
Knowing which audience is looking at any given post mattered more than any single piece of creative — the wrong tone in front of the wrong buyer is the whole risk in a two-sided product like this.

projects.title: Agami Realty
projects.summary: Social content and creative for the Agami Realty brand

projects.title: Celfie Design
projects.summary: Social content and creative for the Celfie Design brand

projects.summary: Social content and creative for the House of D'oro brand

projects.title: Moduli
projects.summary: Social content and creative for the Moduli brand

projects.title: Myonsto
projects.summary: Social content and creative for the Myonsto brand

projects.title: Rise Accelerator
projects.summary: Social content and creative for the Rise Accelerator brand

projects.title: The Complete World
projects.summary: Social content and creative for The Complete World brand

projects.title: Sozo Izakaya
projects.summary: A self-serve Japanese izakaya in Mumbai, styled to feel like Tokyo rather than explain it
body: A self-serve izakaya in Mumbai has one hard job: convincing people used to full table service that ordering and grilling their own food at the table is the better night out, not a downgrade.

The content leaned on atmosphere over explanation — styled to feel like a night in Tokyo rather than a guide to how izakaya dining works — trusting that the mood would do more to sell the format than a how-it-works post ever could.

The real shoot behind this campaign was built around low light, steam and close-up grilling shots specifically, since that texture is what separates 'Japanese restaurant' from 'izakaya' in a diner's mind before they've read a word of copy.
